content: There are no .sbd files in the Thunderbird profile but only .sbd file folders which contain message files ("mailbox" or "mbox" files) A folder containing subfolders in Thunderbird is represented in the profile by an *.sbd folder and a mailbox (mbox) file without extension, having the same name as the .sbd file folder (I neglect the .msf file with the same name since it is an index file). So if you recovered the .sbd folder from your backup you probably should also have recovered the mailbox file having the same name. It is impossible to solve your issue without having a look on this folder hierarchy in the profile folder: please take a screen shot of the contents of the directory where this .sbd folder is located in the profile

content:
aszwarz said
I must confess that I messed up and deleted the mbox files (without extension) by mistake. Doing so removed all the folders that I created.

No, removing an mbox file which is linked with the corresponding .sbd folder doesn't remove the folders you had created. The mbox files of those folders are still present in the .mbox folder! And you only need to create a new empty text file with a simple text editor and give it the same name... and your folders will reappear.

aszwarz said
So, my only issue now (and my original problem) are emails that I stored in these folders/subfolders that now I can't see. I get "No Message Found"

Did you try a R-click on the faulty folder > Properties > Repair Folder ?

aszwarz said
I've searched all around for date restrictions, filters etc and can't come up with anything. Did I mess up again?

As I said in my first answer: "lost message" problems can hardly be solved in classic help forums. One needs to have a look on the screen to be able to search in the Thunderbird profile; sometimes many screen shots can be helpful.` There is an add-on which allows to recover deleted messages: "Undelete" (Recover accidentally deleted messages) https://www.ggbs.de/extensions/Undelete.html but I doubt whether it would be useful in your case

content: Andre, I have plenty of time and you won't waste it! To download the add-on "Undelete" from https://www.ggbs.de/extensions/Undelete.html R-click on "Download latest version" and choose "Save target as..." in the context menu. You'll get a file "Undelete-current.xpi" which you must import in the Add-ons Manager of your Thunderbird: open the Add-ons Manager, click on the cogwheel symbol, select "Install Add-on From File" and navigate to the add-on .xpi file you'd just downloaded....

content:
When you reset your Windows PC, you delete your files.

So, even when you reinstall Thunderbird - the emails where on the PC/ OS you have reseted, when you was using POP3.

So maybe you or your email provider has a backup - that's the only way.

Or when you didn't used POP3, than your email are still on the server of your provider. Look on his Help-Page for a contact form or email-address. He'll need to know your email-address and maybe some further information, like customer number or else.

The alternative would be to complicate to explain here in simple words.

Hope I was helpful (and I'm sorry about your old emails)

content:
Hello there col.


It seems you are unable to find the setting to prevent messages from being left on the server. Could I ask why you uninstalled the previous version of Thunderbird from that PC?

Can you provide more information? Do you use IMAP or POP accounts?



Pop Right click on the email account in the left panel>settings. And now search for the server setting > in there can you search for the settings >leave message on the server>uncheck that option.

The option is there to remember that you also can configure x days to select specific day limit. 


Imap

Imap accounts constantly synchronise with the server, which means that copies are continuously stored on the server. This feature cannot simply be turned on or off. Right-click on your email address and select 'Settings'. Then, under your account, click on 'Server Settings'. Under 'When I delete a message', choose either 'Move it to this folder: Trash' or 'Mark it as deleted'.




Note: Delete permanently To permanently delete messages from the server, go to Server Settings > Message Storage and check 'Empty Trash on Exit'.
creator: Googlethunderbird.

content: Thunderbird is not rejecting your password, your email provider is. That may happen if the account is not set up properly. For example, people sometimes fail to put their full email address into the server settings pane, and that prevents the email provider from being able to confirm the account. You might check with hctx.net to verify that your settings are correct, including port numbers and authentication requirements. Or, you could post screenshots of exactly what is in your server settings pane and SMTP server settings pane to see if anyone on this forum has suggestions. thank you. content: Hi PD, this error is not related to the number of recipients in your email itself. Even when sending to a single recipient, the message “Policy-SCO1 You've reached your sending Limit” comes from your mail server, not from Mozilla Thunderbird. It usually means that your account has hit a temporary sending limit imposed by the email provider, for example due to too many messages sent in a short period, background or stuck messages being sent from the outbox, or security/rate-limiting protections triggered by the server. Sometimes hidden CC/BCC fields, mailing lists, or synced devices can also cause extra outgoing messages without it being obvious. To troubleshoot, check your Outbox and Sent folders for stuck or repeated emails, make sure no automatic CC/BCC rules or templates are adding recipients, and if possible try sending the same email via webmail to see if the issue also occurs there. If it does, the limitation is definitely server-side and not caused by Thunderbird. In that case, the best solution is usually to wait for the temporary limit to reset and contact your email provider to ask specifically about the “Policy-SCO1 sending limit” on your account.

A Google search provides this answer...

(https://www.google.com/search?q=change+where+thunderbird+saves+sent+messages&rlz=1C2VDKB_enGB1120GB1120&num=10&newwindow=1&sca_esv=f1fb8f2ab13df819&sxsrf=ANbL-n4dDWRVyIk875ujzquXiuBkP5UCaA%3A1779651138271&source=hp&ei=QlITaraNDZuvhbIP1e3FmAc&iflsig=AFdpzrgAAAAAahNgUkVxCkfIyq9ze8R0gKH5MAo1vSlQ&ved=0ahUKEwi2tquP1dKUAxWbV0EAHdV2EXMQ4dUDCCU&uact=5&oq=change+where+thunderbird+saves+sent+messages&gs__EFQgPzLKv99aGSBwQ0MS40oAe5qwKyBwQ0MC40uAfZHcIHCTEuMjMuMTguM8gHmwGACAE&sclient=gws-wiz)

Follow these quick steps:

Click the menu icon (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner of Thunderbird and select Account Settings.

In the left-hand panel, click on Copies & Folders directly under the specific email account you want to change.

On the right side, find the When sending messages, automatically: section.

Locate Place a copy in and choose your preferred destination:

Your settings should look like this (mine)... See attached pic 😉

Hope that helps. 😊
